source.title: Machine learning-assisted directed protein evolution with combinatorial libraries

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Directed protein evolution is limited by the high experimental cost of screening large combinatorial libraries. The paper proposes integrating machine learning to predict protein fitness in silico, reducing the number of variants that need to be tested experimentally. The goal is to efficiently navigate sequence space by making multiple mutations simultaneously, avoiding the sequential single-mutation walks of traditional directed evolution. The approach is validated on an empirical fitness landscape (GB1) and applied to engineer enantioselective enzymes for a new-to-nature reaction.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Machine learning is incorporated into directed evolution to reduce experimental effort by screening combinatorial libraries in silico. Validated on GB1 fitness landscape, showing higher fitness than other methods. Applied to evolve enzyme for stereodivergent carbene Si–H insertion, achieving 93% and 79% ee in two rounds. ML enhances quality and diversity of solutions.
process_signals: Problem: high experimental cost of sampling combinatorial sequence space
Solution: ML models trained on tested variants to predict fitness computationally
Validation: GB1 landscape shows ML-guided evolution finds higher fitness
Application: enzyme evolution for enantioselectivity, two rounds, seven mutations fixed
Outcome: 93% and 79% ee, increased throughput and diversity
methods: Machine learning models trained on sequence-function data
In silico screening of combinatorial libraries
Two rounds of evolution with predicted libraries

section_gists.heading: Significance Statement
section_gists.gist: Proposes that ML can decrease expense and improve outcomes of directed evolution. Simulations show greater performance improvement. ML-assisted evolution from a single parent produced enzyme variants for enantioselective new-to-nature reaction. ML efficiently navigates large sequence space and produces diverse solutions.
process_signals: Motivation: proteins often need engineering for new roles
Hypothesis: ML reduces experimental burden and improves results
Evidence: simulations show better performance; wet-lab achieves enantioselective variants
Benefit: explores multiple mutations simultaneously, finds diverse solutions

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Directed evolution is powerful but screening is a bottleneck. In silico methods like molecular dynamics are resource-intensive. Machine learning is an emerging alternative that builds models from data. This work uses ML to enhance directed evolution with combinatorial libraries, enabling multiple mutations per round. Approach validated on GB1 dataset and applied to engineer enzyme for stereodivergent C–Si bond formation.
process_signals: Problem: screening is resource-intensive bottleneck in directed evolution
Existing alternatives: MD simulations require CPU hours and mechanistic understanding
ML advantage: data-driven, efficient, versatile
Previous ML in protein engineering: identified beneficial mutations and optimal fragments
This work: ML-assisted directed evolution with combinatorial libraries, multiple mutations per round
Hypothesis: ML can traverse fitness landscapes more efficiently by modeling epistasis
Validation plan: GB1 empirical landscape (4 positions, 160k variants)
Application plan: enzyme for new-to-nature C–Si bond formation
methods: Combinatorial library sampling
Machine learning models trained on sequence-function data
Predicted restricted libraries enriched in high fitness

section_gists.heading: Directed evolution and machine learning
section_gists.gist: In ML-assisted directed evolution, sequence-function data from all variants train a panel of models. Best models predict fitness of all possible sequences, and a restricted library of top predicted variants is constructed and screened. This allows exploring full combinatorial space of multiple positions simultaneously, unlike sequential single mutations or recombination of top mutations. Fig. 1 illustrates the approach for four positions.
process_signals: Workflow: train models on random sample of combinatorial library (input library)
Use models to predict fitness of all possible variants
Construct predicted library of top-ranked variants using degenerate codons
Screen predicted library experimentally
Best variant becomes parent for next round with new positions
Advantage over single-mutation walk: explores epistatic interactions, larger steps in sequence space
Advantage over recombination: provides variability estimates at each position, not just top mutations
methods: Panel of models: linear, kernel, neural network, ensemble
Model selection based on accuracy
Degenerate codon design for predicted library (SwiftLib)

section_gists.heading: Validation on an Empirical Fitness Landscape
section_gists.gist: Simulated directed evolution on GB1 fitness landscape (4 positions, 149,361 measured variants). Compared single-mutation walk, recombination, and ML-assisted approach with equal screening burden (570 variants). ML used 470 random training variants, predicted top 100. ML-assisted approach reached higher fitness more frequently: 17% reached fitness 7, 8% reached fitness 8, vs. 7% and 0% for single-mutation walk. Recombination performed intermediately. ML avoids low-fitness peaks.
process_signals: Dataset: GB1 binding to IgG-Fc, 4 positions, fitness max 8.76, 19.7% at 0
Single-mutation walk: greedy, deterministic, 570 variants screened (3-fold oversampling for 95% coverage)
Recombination: random sample, recombine top 3 mutations, max library 81, alternative recombination worse
ML-assisted: 470 training, 100 predicted, total 570; shallow neural networks, 4-fold CV based on Pearson's r
Results: ML-assisted distribution shifted to higher fitness; 17% reach fitness 7, 8% reach 8
Single-mutation walk: 7% reach 7, 0% reach 8; recombination: 10% reach 7, 2% reach 8
ML avoids low-fitness peaks (0% at fitness 0 vs. 6% for single-mutation)
Design choices: training/prediction split based on plate size, could be optimized (Fig. S1A)
methods: Shallow neural networks with randomized hyperparameter search
4-fold cross-validation based on Pearson's r
Model selection based on error on 1000 held-out variants
Prediction of top 100 sequences
metrics: Fitness (enrichment of folded protein bound to antibody)
failures_or_limits: Design choices (number of training vs. predicted variants) could be improved for higher expected fitness (Fig. S1A)
Alternative recombination strategy performed worse (Fig. S1B)

section_gists.heading: Application to Evolution of Enantiodivergent Enzyme Activity
section_gists.gist: Applied ML-assisted directed evolution to engineer Rma NOD for enantioselective C–Si bond formation. Starting parent: Y32K V97L (76% ee (S)). Two sets of positions: Set I (K32, F46, L56, L97) near active site; Set II (P49, R51, I53) near substrate channel. Used NDT codons for input libraries to maximize diversity. Round 1: 124 sequence-function pairs trained models, predicted libraries of 90 variants each for (S)- and (R)-enantiomers. Identified VCHV (93% ee (S)) and GSSG (62% ee (R)). Round 2: used VCHV and GSSG as parents for Set II. From predicted libraries, obtained variants with 93% ee (S) and 79% ee (R). Total 805 variants tested over 7 positions. VCHV was less selective in final validation than initial screen.
process_signals: Goal: engineer enzyme for both enantiomers of new-to-nature C–Si bond formation
Parent choice: Rma NOD Y32K V97L, 76% ee (S), thermostable, less enantiopreference than previous enzyme
Position selection: Set I near active site, Set II near substrate channel
Library design: NDT codons for input libraries to maximize diversity and avoid overrepresentation
Round 1: 124 input variants, models trained, 90 predicted variants screened per enantiomer
Round 1 results: VCHV (93% ee (S)), GSSG (62% ee (R)); VCHV less selective in final validation
Round 2: VCHV and GSSG as parents for Set II; predicted libraries screened
Round 2 results: 93% ee (S) and 79% ee (R) final variants
Total: 445 sequence-function relationships for training, 360 predicted variants tested, 805 total over 7 positions
Throughput limitation: one 96-well plate per predicted library; more throughput could improve results (Fig. S1A)
Failed sequencing reads reduced input data below two full plates
methods: NDT codon mutagenesis for input libraries
SwiftLib for degenerate codon design of predicted libraries
Whole-cell reactions, SFC chiral separation for ee measurement
Panel of models trained, top models used for prediction
failures_or_limits: VCHV was most selective in initial screen but less selective in final validation
Failed sequencing reads reduced input data
Limited throughput (one plate per predicted library) may limit reaching optimum

section_gists.heading: Machine Learning Identifies Diverse Improved Sequences
section_gists.gist: ML-assisted directed evolution identifies diverse improved sequences, not just single best. Tables show top variants from input and predicted libraries for Sets I and II. Predicted library variants contain mutations not present in top input variants, demonstrating non-obvious solutions. Evolved variants also show increased cellular activity. Diversity provides multiple solutions with different properties (e.g., activity).
process_signals: Benefit: ML identifies diverse set of improved sequences, not just top one
Diversity useful for other design requirements (activity, substrate tolerance, IP)
Tables 1 and 2: top variants in input vs. predicted libraries
Predicted library variants have mutations not in top input variants (non-obvious)
Example: top (S)-selective predicted variants have mutations at positions 32,46,56 not in top input
Evolved variants show increased cellular activity over starting variant (2.2-3.2 fold)
Activity increase may contribute to selectivity by overcoming background
Two most (S)-selective variants differ <1% ee but one has higher activity

section_gists.heading: Machine Learning Predicts Regions of Sequence Space Enriched in Function
section_gists.gist: ML-assisted approach focuses experiments on regions of sequence space enriched in desired function. Visualized as 1D fitness distributions of input vs. predicted libraries. Predicted libraries are shifted toward higher fitness (right for (S), left for (R)). ML benefits more pronounced in Set II (fewer positions, more data). Predicted library sizes: Set I 864 (S), 630 (R); Set II 192 (S), 90 (R). 90 variants tested per library.
process_signals: Alternative analysis: focus on library fitness distributions, not individual best variants
Visualization: kernel density estimates of fitness for input (lighter) and predicted (darker) libraries
Predicted libraries shifted toward desired enantioselectivity
Random mutations biased toward parent selectivity
ML benefits greater in Set II due to fewer positions and more data per position
Predicted library sizes: Set I 864 (S), 630 (R); Set II 192 (S), 90 (R)
90 variants tested per predicted library; Set II had redundancy due to small theoretical size
methods: Kernel density estimation for fitness distributions
Predicted library construction using degenerate codons

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: ML screens full recombination library in silico, reducing in vitro screening. Evolved single parent to both enantiomers of new-to-nature reaction. Models epistasis directly by sampling combinatorial space. ML enables larger steps in sequence space, avoiding negative epistasis and indirect paths. Diverse solutions found (e.g., position 49 tolerates Pro, Tyr, Arg). Approach differs from Gaussian process-based probability of improvement. Multiple model types tested. Future improvements: more data, better physical descriptors, embedded representations. ML leverages limited data without detailed mechanistic understanding.
process_signals: Key achievement: rapid evolution of enantioselective variants from single parent
Advantage over ProSAR: models epistasis directly, not just single mutations
ML increases effective throughput, enables larger steps, avoids negative epistasis
Diverse solutions: position 49 tolerates multiple amino acids with minimal ee loss
Comparison to Saito et al.: they use Gaussian processes for probability of improvement; this work identifies optimal variants directly
Model selection: multiple types tested because best model unknown a priori
Limitations: predictive accuracy could be improved with more data or better simulations
Future: deep mutational scanning datasets for validation, better physical descriptors, embedded representations
Overall: ML leverages limited experimental resources without mechanistic understanding
methods: Panel of models: linear, kernel, neural network, ensemble
Model selection based on accuracy
Combinatorial library sampling
failures_or_limits: Predictive accuracy limited by data quantity and model abstraction
More data or computationally expensive simulations could improve accuracy
Current implementation is baseline; future work can incorporate better descriptors

section_gists.heading: Approach Validation on an Empirical Fitness Landscape
section_gists.gist: GB1 fitness landscape: 149,361 measured variants, 4 positions. Simulated three approaches: single-mutation walk (greedy, 4 rounds, 570 variants), recombination (random 527 variants, recombine top 3, avg 570 variants), ML-assisted (470 training, 100 predicted, total 570). ML used shallow neural networks, 4-fold CV based on Pearson's r, model selection on 1000 held-out variants. Process repeated with varying training sizes (Fig. S1A).
process_signals: Dataset: only measured sequences used, no imputation
Single-mutation walk: deterministic, 19N variants per round, 4 rounds, 570 total (3-fold oversampling for 95% coverage)
Recombination: 527 random variants, top 3 recombined, average 570 over 10,000 simulations
ML-assisted: 470 training, 100 predicted; shallow neural networks with randomized hyperparameter search
Model evaluation: 4-fold CV Pearson's r, error on 1000 held-out variants
Predicted library size 100 chosen to match 96-well plate capacity
Total screening burden equalized at 570 variants
Sensitivity analysis: varied training size to simulate lower accuracy (Fig. S1A)
methods: Shallow neural networks
Randomized hyperparameter search
4-fold cross-validation with Pearson's r
Model selection based on held-out error

section_gists.heading: Library Cloning, Expression, and Characterization of Rma NOD
section_gists.gist: Rma NOD gene cloned into pET22b(+). Libraries constructed with degenerate codons via SwiftLib. Expression in E. coli BL21 in 96 deep-well plates. Activity measured by whole-cell reactions with Me-EDA and PhMe2SiH, anaerobic, 6 hr. Enantiomeric excess measured by SFC with chiral column.
process_signals: Cloning: gBlock into pET22b(+)
Library construction: PCR, Gibson assembly, degenerate codons from SwiftLib
Codon distribution verified by sequencing (Supp Fig. 2)
Expression: 96 deep-well plates, 1 mL HyperBroth, E. coli BL21, induced with IPTG and heme precursor
Activity assay: whole cells, 10 mM substrates, anaerobic, 6 hr, cyclohexane extraction
ee measurement: SFC with Chiralcel OD-H column, 95% CO2, 5% isopropanol, 3 min
methods: SwiftLib for degenerate codon design
Whole-cell biotransformation
Supercritical fluid chromatography (SFC) for chiral analysis

section_gists.heading: Rma NOD Model Training and Prediction Testing
section_gists.gist: Sequence-function pairs from screening used to train panel of models (KNN, linear, trees, random forests, MLP) with default hyperparameters in scikit-learn. Top 3 model types selected, gridsearch CV for hyperparameters. Top 3 hyperparameter sets for top 3 models used to predict top 1000 sequences. Degenerate codons designed by SwiftLib to encode frequent amino acids. 90 random variants from predicted library tested in vitro. Random sampling differs from GB1 where all sequences enumerated.
process_signals: Data: sequence-function pairs from screening, available on ProtaBank
Model panel: KNN, linear (ARD, Bayesian Ridge, Elastic Net, Lasso LARS, Ridge), decision trees, random forests (AdaBoost, Bagging, Gradient Boosting), MLP
Default hyperparameters initially, then top 3 models selected
Gridsearch cross-validation for hyperparameter optimization
Top 3 hyperparameter sets for top 3 model types used for prediction
Prediction: top 1000 sequences ranked by frequency across models
Library design: SwiftLib identifies degenerate codons for frequent amino acids
Testing: 90 random variants from predicted library screened
Random sampling means optimal predicted sequence may not be tested, but fitness distributions can be generated
methods: scikit-learn models
Gridsearch cross-validation
SwiftLib for degenerate codon design
Random sampling of predicted library for experimental testing
failures_or_limits: Random sampling of predicted library may miss optimal sequence
Model accuracy limited by data quantity and default hyperparameters initially

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The paper hypothesizes that machine learning can effectively reduce the experimental burden of directed evolution by predicting fitness of combinatorial libraries in silico, enabling multiple mutations per round and more efficient navigation of sequence space.
It posits that ML models trained on a random sample of a combinatorial library can capture epistatic interactions and guide the construction of smaller, enriched libraries.
The approach is expected to find higher fitness variants and more diverse solutions compared to traditional single-mutation walks or recombination strategies.
The wet-lab application aims to demonstrate that ML-assisted evolution can rapidly engineer enantioselective enzymes for a new-to-nature reaction from a single parent, achieving both enantiomers.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How does the performance of ML-assisted evolution scale with library size and number of positions?
What is the optimal ratio of training to predicted variants for different landscapes?
How transferable are models between different protein systems or functions?
What is the impact of different model types and hyperparameters on prediction accuracy and evolutionary outcomes?
How does the approach handle highly rugged or deceptive fitness landscapes?
What are the limits of using NDT codons for input library diversity, and how does it affect model training?
Can the approach be integrated with higher-throughput screening methods to further improve outcomes?
How robust is the method to experimental noise and missing data (e.g., failed sequencing reads)?

events.summary: Combinatorial sequence space is expensive to sample experimentally, motivating in silico screening.
Machine learning models trained on tested variants can computationally test sequence space, reducing experimental effort.
The approach uses a random sample of a combinatorial library to train models, then predicts a restricted library enriched in high fitness.
Shallow neural networks were trained with 470 randomly-selected input variants and used to predict 100 variants for testing.
Simulated directed evolution on GB1 fitness landscape compared single-mutation walk, recombination, and ML-assisted approach with equal screening burden (570 variants).
Design choices such as the number of training vs. predicted variants could be improved for higher expected fitness.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, significance, introduction, and part of results including validation on GB1 landscape. Events focus on problem, hypothesis, design, computation, experiment, analysis, and limitation.

events.summary: ML-assisted evolution reaches global optimum fitness in 8.2% of simulations vs. 4.9% for single-mutant walk and 4.0% for recombination.
ML approach requires about 30% fewer variants to achieve results similar to single-mutant walk.
ML approach has higher expected fitness (6.42) vs. single-mutant walk (5.41) and recombination (5.93).
ML model accuracy can be low (Pearson's r = 0.41) but still sufficient to guide evolution.
Round 1: From 124 sequence-function pairs, models predicted libraries; screening 90 variants each identified VCHV (93% ee (S)) and GSSG (62% ee (R)).
Round 2: Using VCHV and GSSG as parents, predicted libraries yielded variants with 93% ee (S) and 79% ee (R).
Total experimental effort: 445 sequence-function pairs for training, 360 predicted variants tested, 805 total over 7 positions.
VCHV was most selective in initial screen but less selective in final validation.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key validation results on GB1 landscape and detailed experimental application for enzyme evolution, including specific metrics and limitations.

events.summary: Proposes an alternative analysis focusing on library fitness distributions rather than individual best variants.
Visualizes input and predicted library fitness distributions as kernel density estimates, showing shift toward desired enantioselectivity.
Notes that random mutations are biased toward parent selectivity, and ML focuses predictions on high-fitness regions.
Reports predicted library sizes and number of variants tested, noting ML benefits more pronounced in Position Set II.
Discusses how ML enables larger steps in sequence space by modeling epistasis and avoiding negative epistatic effects.
Highlights that ML-assisted evolution finds diverse solutions, e.g., position 49 tolerates multiple amino acids with minimal ee loss.
Acknowledges that predictive accuracy could be improved by more data or computationally expensive simulations.
Describes model training and prediction testing: panel of models, gridsearch CV, top 1000 sequences, degenerate codon design, random sampling of 90 variants.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains analysis of fitness distributions, discussion of ML benefits, limitations, and detailed methods for model training and prediction testing.

events.summary: Model training uses a panel of regressors from scikit-learn, selecting top 3 model types by Leave-One-Out cross-validation Pearson correlation, then gridsearch hyperparameter optimization to select 9 models total.
Predicted libraries are designed by interpreting model predictions as amino acid frequencies in the top 1000 sequences, then encoding frequent amino acids with degenerate codons via SwiftLib.
The prediction interpretation step loses exact sequence combinations, potentially including non-optimal variants, but balances experimental costs.

Protein expression and biocatalytic reaction protocol: cells grown in 96 deep-well plates, induced, and whole-cell reactions with substrates Me-EDA and PhMe2SiH under anaerobic conditions for 4 hours.
Enantiomeric excess measured by supercritical fluid chromatography (SFC) with chiral column, using automatic integration.
Starting variant Y32K V97L chosen for higher activity over wild-type to improve data reproducibility, despite slightly lower enantioselectivity.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for model training, prediction interpretation, experimental protocols, and model performance statistics, which are core to the paper's process.

events.summary: Analysis of amino acid frequencies in top predicted sequences to guide degenerate codon design.
Design of predicted libraries using degenerate codons based on top model predictions.
Analysis of the effect of training data size on ML-assisted evolution performance.
Analysis of predicted vs measured enantioselectivity for predicted libraries.
Comparison of input and predicted library amino acid compositions to assess cloning biases.
Limitation of ProSAR-like method on landscapes with epistasis.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains supplementary analyses and method details, including amino acid frequency analysis, model ensemble design, performance comparisons, and limitations of ProSAR.

events.summary: Comparison of library coverage requirements for single-mutation walk under different codon assumptions.
Justification for using 570 variants as baseline for single-mutation walk comparison.
Chiral SFC analysis of racemic and enzymatic organosilicon products to determine enantiomeric excess.
methods_or_tools: Chiral SFC with Chiralcel OD-H column

events.summary: Statistical validation of improved activity and enantioselectivity of evolved variants over starting variants.
methods_or_tools: conversion of ee to ΔΔG via ln(R/S)

events.summary: Model performance and selection for Set I and Set II using leave-one-out Pearson r.
Acknowledgment that the method does not guarantee identifying variants better than the best in the input library.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains supplementary information: library coverage calculations, chiral SFC traces, statistical validation of improved variants, and model performance details. It provides supporting evidence for the main claims but does not introduce new core research events.

events.summary: Model selection for Set 2(S): top models chosen based on cross-validation performance.
Model selection for Set 2(R): top models chosen based on cross-validation performance.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains tables of model performance and lists of selected models for different sets, which are part of the computational process but do not describe new experimental or design events beyond model selection.
This chunk contains only model hyperparameter listings and supplemental references, with no research evolution events.

evidence_bank.quote: Combinatorial sequence space can be quite expensive to sample experimentally
machine learning models trained on tested variants provide a fast method for testing sequence space computationally

data from a random sample of the combinatorial library, the input library, are used to train machine learning models. These models are used to predict a smaller set of variants, the predicted library, which can be encoded with degenerate codons to test experimentally
shallow neural networks were trained with 470 randomly-selected input variants, from which 100 predictions were tested
we compare the final fitnesses reached by simulated directed evolution with and without machine learning, based on testing the same number of variants
these design choices could be improved to increase the expected fitness improvement

machine learning-assisted evolution reaches the global optimum fitness value in 8.2% of 600 simulations, compared to 4.9% of all starting sequences reaching the same value through a single-mutant walk and 4.0% of simulated recombination runs.
the machine-learning approach requires about 30% fewer variants to achieve final results similar to the single-mutant walk with this analysis.
the machine learning approach has an expected fitness value of 6.42, compared to 5.41 and 5.93 for the single step walk and recombination, respectively.

the accuracy of the machine learning models as determined on a test set of 1000 random variants not found in the training set can be quite low (Pearson's r = 0.41 with stdev 0.17). However, this level of accuracy as measured by Pearson's r appears to be sufficient to guide evolution.
From 124 sequence–function relationships sampled randomly, models were trained to predict a restricted set of selective variants.

From the predicted libraries for both enantiomers, two variants, called VCHV (93 % ee) and GSSG (62 % ee) for their amino acids at positions 32, 46, 56, and 97, were identified by screening 90 variants for each.
From those predicted libraries, we obtained two variants with measured enantioselectivities of 93% and 79% ee for the (S)- and (R)-enantiomers, respectively.

These two enantioselective enzymes were achieved after obtaining 445 sequence-function relationships for model training and testing an additional 360 predicted variants, for a total of 805 variants tested experimentally covering 7 positions
VCHV was the most selective variant in the initial screen, but was less selective in final validation.
we caution against focusing solely on examples of individual variants with higher fitness and propose an alternative analysis.
an alternative representation of a library is a 1-dimensional distribution of fitness values sampled at random for each encoded library.
Each subplot in Fig. 5 shows both the input and predicted (output) library as kernel density estimates in each round of evolution for R- and S-selectivity as fitness.
the distribution of random mutations made in the input libraries is shifted toward the parent
the machine-learning algorithm is able to focus its predictions on areas of sequence space that are enriched in high fitness

the machine learning algorithms appear to have more pronounced benefits in Position Set II, likely due to the smaller number of positions explored and larger number of sequence-function relationships obtained.
we modeled epistatic interactions at the mutated positions by sampling the combinatorial sequence space directly and incorporating models with nonlinear interactions.
Machine learning increases effective throughput by providing an efficient computational method for estimating desired properties of all possible proteins in a large library.
we can take larger steps through sequence space by identifying combinations of beneficial mutations, circumventing the need for indirect paths
proline is conserved at residue 49 in two of the most (S)-selective variants.
tyrosine and arginine are also tolerated at position 49 with less than 1% loss in enantioselectivity.
This suggests that there are diverse solutions in protein space for specific properties

While predictive accuracy could be improved by more computationally-expensive simulations or by collecting more data for machine learning, improved variants can already be identified by sampling from a space predicted to be dense in higher fitness variants.
gridsearch cross-validation was used to identify the optimal hyperparameters.
The top 3 hyperparameter sets for the top 3 model types were used to identify the top 1000 sequences in each predicted library.
The 3 model types with highest Pearson correlation from a Leave-One-Out cross validation (LOO CV) with default hyperparameters were selected for gridsearch hyperparameter optimization.
From this gridsearch, the 3 sets of hyperparameters with highest LOO CV Pearson correlation were selected, for a total of 9 models

we interpret our models' predictions by the frequency of each amino acid's occurrence in a top fraction (the top 1000) of the library, which we are able to encode efficiently with degenerate codon libraries.
The top amino acids at each position are encoded by degenerate codons identified by SwiftLib
At this step, the exact combinations predicted by the machine learning models are lost, and instead interpreted as frequencies at individual amino acid positions to encode with degenerate codons.
While the optimal combinations of amino acids identified by the model are retained in this library, there may be non-optimal combinations that result from this procedure.
Four replicates of each well position were made to minimize variability in cell culture and maximize accuracy for downstream modeling.
The reaction plate was covered with a foil cover (USA scientific) and shaken at 1000 rpm for 4 hours.
Automatic integration was performed in ChemStation.
we started with a previously-engineered variant, Y32K V97L, for its significantly higher activity, which we hypothesized would make data collection more reproducible.
The amino acids occurring significantly more frequently in the top 1000 sequences are then [Y, N, R, G], [V, F, I], and [I, V, L, S, F].
This process is repeated for the top 3 models determined by default hyperparameters, and then 3 hyperparameter sets are used for each optimal, for a total of 9 models

With 570 sequences measured (in black), the machine learning-assisted evolution approach reaches the global optimum fitness value in 8.4% of simulations, compared to 4.9% of all starting sequences (in blue).
The predicted versus measured values for sequence-verified variants in the predicted libraries are shown for each library.
heat maps of encoded amino acids are shown for each round comparing the input and predicted libraries.

in our recombination landscape of a small number of positions with known epistasis (nonlinear effects), this approach should not be expected to find optimal solutions (and does not outperform other methods tested).
Protein engineers often aim for 95% library coverage (9, 10), or a 95% probability of seeing a particular variant in the library.
Assuming equal frequency of each amino acid, this number is roughly 3-fold the library size, which is often used in practice (9).
From a protein engineer's perspective, a comparison to 644 variants is likely the most pertinent in Supp Figure 1.
However, to provide DE alone with a stronger baseline, we have used 570 variants, obtained from applying the 3-fold oversampling rule (9) to 10 libraries containing 19 desired variants each.

In any case, we empirically observe that the single-mutation walk performs similarly to the ML approach at 300-400 variants (Supp Figure 1), which is significantly less than any of the numbers presented here.
All the ee values of synthesized organosilicon products were determined using automatic peak integration from chiral SFC.
Chiralcel OD-H (4.6 mm x 25 cm), 5% isopropanol in CO₂, 3 mL/min, 210 nm
Activity is significantly improved over starting variant KFLL
Enantioselectivity in Set II is significantly improved over starting variant VCHV
Enantioselectivity in Set II is significantly improved over starting variant GSSG
The p-values obtained from Welch's t-test are shown below.
The LOO Pearson r of the regressors with default hyperparameters and the models ultimately selected are shown for each round.

we would like to reiterate that while we have shown this method is more likely to find better variants on an empirical method, this method does not guarantee identifying protein variants that are better than the best identified in the input library.
# Set 2(S) Models Selected
# Set 2(R) Models Selected

Carbon–silicon bond formation is a new-to-nature enzyme reaction (20), and Rma NOD with mutations Y32K and V97L catalyzes this reaction with 76% ee for the (S)-enantiomer in whole-cell reactions (SI Appendix, Table 1).
In the first round, Rma NOD Y32K V97L (76 % ee) was used as a parent for NDT mutagenesis at the Set I positions.

Although WT has slightly lower enantioselectivity (and thus may reach both enantiomers more easily), we started with a previously-engineered variant, Y32K V97L, for its significantly higher activity, which we hypothesized would make data collection more reproducible.
5 mM isopropyl $\beta$ -D-1-thiogalactopyranoside and supplemented with 1 mM 5-aminolevulinic acid to increase heme production.
Mutations that improve selectivity for the $(S)$ -enantiomer appear in the background of $[32V, 46C, 56H, 97V (VCHV)]$ and for the $(R)$ -enantiomer are in $[32G, 46S, 56S, 97G (GSSG)]$ .
Therefore, for 19 mutations away from one of the 20 canonical amino acids, $19 \times 3 = 57$ variants are needed for roughly 95% coverage.
The relative product activity was measured using 10 mM Me-EDA and 10 mM PhMe $_2$ SiH with whole cells resuspended in 400 uL nitrogen-free M9-N buffer, pH 7.4 (47.

With 570 total variants (SI Appendix, Library Coverage) this leaves 470 variants for the input library in step I for an equal screening burden, assuming 95% coverage of 19 mutations from wild type at each position.

Thus we can take larger steps through sequence space by identifying combinations of beneficial mutations, circumventing the need for indirect paths (16) or alterations of the nature of selection (31), and potentially avoiding negative epistatic effects resulting from the accumulation of large numbers of mutations (33) that require reversion later in the evolution (34).
Activity increase over the starting variant, 32K, 46F, 56L, 97L (KFLL), is shown for the final variants.

The improvement in total activity measured in whole cells compared to the starting variant (32K, 46F, 56L, 97L) obtained after two rounds of machine learning-assisted directed evolution is also shown in Table 2.

Assuming each amino acid occurs with equal frequency and that the library has complete coverage, applying the 3-fold oversampling rule to obtain roughly 95% library coverage (18, 19) results in a total of 570 variants screened (SI Appendix, Library Coverage).

A typical balance between balancing the degenerate codon complexity and amino acid coverage that protein engineers employ is the use of NDT/VHG/TGG codons, also known as the 22c-trick (10), in which methionine occurs 1/22 times for $N \approx 64.4$ , or 644 variants over 10 libraries.
The least frequent amino acid encoded with these codons (methionine) occurs at a frequency of 1/32, requiring $N \approx 94.4$ , or 944 variants.
Assuming equal representation of the 19 codons gives $N \approx 55.4$ , or 554 variants for the 10 libraries needed.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Directed protein evolution is limited by the high experimental cost of screening large combinatorial libraries. The paper proposes integrating machine learning to predict protein fitness in silico, reducing the number of variants that need to be tested experimentally.

canonical_events.summary: Combinatorial sequence space is expensive to sample experimentally, motivating in silico screening.
Machine learning models trained on tested variants can computationally test sequence space, reducing experimental effort.
The approach uses a random sample of a combinatorial library to train models, then predicts a restricted library enriched in high fitness.
Shallow neural networks were trained with 470 randomly-selected input variants and used to predict 100 variants for testing.
Simulated directed evolution on GB1 fitness landscape compared single-mutation walk, recombination, and ML-assisted approach with equal screening burden (570 variants).
ML-assisted evolution reaches global optimum fitness in 8.2% of simulations vs. 4.9% for single-mutant walk and 4.0% for recombination.
ML approach requires about 30% fewer variants to achieve results similar to single-mutant walk.
ML approach has higher expected fitness (6.42) vs. single-mutant walk (5.41) and recombination (5.93).
ML model accuracy can be low (Pearson's r = 0.41) but still sufficient to guide evolution.
Round 1: From 124 sequence-function pairs, models predicted libraries; screening 90 variants each identified VCHV (93% ee (S)) and GSSG (62% ee (R)).
Round 2: Using VCHV and GSSG as parents, predicted libraries yielded variants with 93% ee (S) and 79% ee (R).
Total experimental effort: 445 sequence-function pairs for training, 360 predicted variants tested, 805 total over 7 positions.
VCHV was most selective in initial screen but less selective in final validation.
ML-assisted evolution finds diverse solutions, e.g., position 49 tolerates multiple amino acids with minimal ee loss.
Predictive accuracy could be improved by more data or computationally expensive simulations.

edges.reason: The problem of expensive experimental sampling motivates the hypothesis that ML can reduce effort.
The hypothesis leads to the design of using random samples to train models and predict enriched libraries.
The design is implemented by training shallow neural networks on 470 variants and predicting 100.
The computational setup is used in simulated directed evolution on GB1 landscape.
The simulation results show ML reaches global optimum more often.
Further analysis shows ML requires fewer variants for similar results.
Expected fitness is higher for ML approach.
Even with low model accuracy, ML can guide evolution effectively.
The validation on GB1 supports applying ML to enzyme evolution in Round 1.
Round 2 uses the best variants from Round 1 as parents.
Total experimental effort is summarized after two rounds.
VCHV's selectivity dropped in final validation, indicating a limitation.
Diverse solutions were found, such as tolerance at position 49.
Despite success, predictive accuracy remains a limitation.

must_cover_points: Hypothesis that ML can reduce experimental effort
Design of training on random sample and predicting enriched library
GB1 simulation setup with equal screening burden
ML reaches global optimum more often (8.2% vs 4.9% and 4.0%)
ML requires 30% fewer variants for similar results
Higher expected fitness with ML (6.42 vs 5.41 and 5.93)
Low model accuracy (Pearson's r=0.41) still sufficient
Round 1 enzyme evolution: VCHV (93% ee S) and GSSG (62% ee R)
Round 2 enzyme evolution: 93% ee S and 79% ee R
Total experimental effort: 805 variants over 7 positions
Limitation: VCHV less selective in final validation
Limitation: predictive accuracy could be improved
